_note: Rough buildout from mattfryed.com/hoverburger + mattfryed.com/arcade-games. A selected run of in-studio arcade cabinets (HOVERBURGER, Hair Nah, Kung Fu Kickball). Copy + credits are Matt's own; images referenced by URL from his Squarespace CDN (swap to repo/own-host later). Node geometry + colours match the live homepage roster (P-06). Sample-grade pending Matt's review.
name: SELECTED ARCADE

intro.title: CLASSIFICATION
intro.body:
A selected run of arcade cabinets designed and fabricated in-studio — HOVERBURGER, Hair Nah, and Kung Fu Kickball. Each is a ground-up enclosure built around another studio's game, tuned to its world and the venue it lives in.

The through-line is the cabinet as a designed object: form, graphics, controls, and secondary screens integrated into a single piece that has to hold a crowd.

nodes.label: Engineering & Fabrication
nodes.blurb: The in-studio cabinet practice — three ground-up enclosures across three years.

columns.name: Hoverburger
columns.body: Original two-screen upright. Arcade cabinet 2023 · cabinet design, fabrication & game design. A MAZE Awards Berlin honoree, 2023.

columns.name: Hair Nah · Kung Fu Kickball
columns.body: Hair Nah (2021–2023) now at the Smithsonian and the Video Game Hall of Fame at The Strong. Kung Fu Kickball — an alternate cabinet for the Moxy Hotel, Williamsburg.

blocks.title: THE PRACTICE
blocks.body: Each cabinet is fabricated in-studio as a one-off: CNC carcass, custom panels, marquee, and control deck, built around whatever the game needs. Integrating secondary screens into the form — without breaking the silhouette — is the recurring design problem these builds solve.

images.caption: Kung Fu Kickball — Moxy Hotel, Williamsburg.
Kung Fu Kickball in the lobby.

nodes.label: Cabinet Design
nodes.blurb: HOVERBURGER — drawn from Tempest and VEC9, with a foldable Burglet version.

blocks.title: HOVERBURGER
blocks.body: You operate a burger-styled spacecraft in a bid to lure the Space Ram away from devouring Earth. The cabinet was drawn with influence from Tempest, Solar Jetman (the in-game craft), and VEC9 — getting the secondary screens to integrate with the form and the dev work was the main goal and the main challenge. It also comes in a foldable, overhead-compartment-sized Burglet version.

nodes.label: Graphic Design
nodes.blurb: Marquee art, one-sheets, and cabinet graphics tuned to each title.

blocks.title: CABINET GRAPHICS
blocks.body: Marquees, one-sheets, and side art carry each game's world onto its enclosure. Hair Nah pairs Momo Pixel's art and concept with a cabinet built to put it in front of museum audiences.

blocks.title: HAIR NAH
blocks.alt: Hair Nah — game art by Momo Pixel
blocks.caption: Hair Nah — art & concept by Momo Pixel. Now at the Smithsonian and The Strong.
Kung Fu Kickball — cabinet for the Moxy.

nodes.label: Interface Design
nodes.blurb: Control layouts and the secondary-screen integration that signs these builds.

blocks.title: CONTROLS & SCREENS
blocks.body: Control decks are laid out per game and wired to the cabinet's secondary screens — the integration of those extra displays into the form, design, and dev work is the detail that defines the Hoverburger build.

blocks.caption: Hoverburger — controls and integrated secondary screens.
